kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: Latest PUBLISHED official is Tue 14-Jul (Westmetall): cash $3,171.00, +$32.50 (+1.04%) — a recovery high struck under the full blockade regime. Westmetall has NOT yet tabled the 15-Jul ring; on live/CFD basis the 3M eased ~0.9% Wednesday, so this official pre-dates Wed's disinflation prints and the pullback. ~17.7% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 14-Jul official $3,167.50 (+$20.50, +0.65%); cash–3M held +$3.50 BACKWARDATION. But Wednesday's live 3M/CFD slipped to ~$3,147.50 (−0.88%), giving back most of the Tuesday bounce as CPI+PPI disinflation and record China exports weighed. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382); $3,050 the structural line. The 15-Jul official (pending on Westmetall) is the tell on whether the backwardation trend survives.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 14-Jul official 284,600 t (−1,625 on the day) — the latest published figure; the unbroken drawdown since early April (509,250 t on 02-Jan → 284,600, −44.1% YTD) leaves the thinnest visible cover of the cycle. Prompt tightness (backwardation) persists even as the flat price wobbles.

kpi_cards.label: BRENT ($/bbl)
kpi_cards.delta: Brent settled ~$84.73 on Wed (essentially flat, +0.01%) after Monday's +9.6% best-day-since-2020, holding gains on a second US strike wave and the Iranian-port blockade; live ~$85.6 into Thursday. Energy is the largest smelter cost line — the war premium in oil is not unwinding.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: Tue 14-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall): $3,171.00 vs 13-Jul $3,138.50 = +$32.50 (+1.04%) — a recovery high on the official series. As of this 16-Jul run Westmetall has NOT posted the 15-Jul official, so the latest published official is unchanged from yesterday; the live 3M/CFD eased ~0.9% Wednesday, so the confirmed official now pre-dates Wednesday's PPI and the pullback. avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 14-Jul official $3,167.50 vs 13-Jul $3,147.00 (+$20.50, +0.65%). Wednesday's live 3M/CFD close printed ~$3,147.50 (−0.88%, TradingEconomics), partly reversing Tuesday's gain — the 15-Jul official is pending on Westmetall. Resistance stack: $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382 of the June–July decline); a close above $3,260 upgrades the technical toward $3,330–3,400. avg = June-2026 3M mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: BACKWARDATION (+$3.50): cash $3,171.00 over 3M $3,167.50 on the latest published (14-Jul) official — Monday's −$8.50 contango flipped back on the first official struck under the full two-sided regime, with stock at 284,600 t (−44.1% YTD). The physical front end confirmed the war story; the open question is whether the 15-Jul official (not yet on Westmetall) BUILDS the backwardation or fades with Wednesday's softer flat price.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t (S&P Global Platts, assessed 06-Jul) — a decade high (+12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353). Spot has printed BELOW settlement ($355, SMM 10-Jul). What arrests the slide before Q4 talks (~Sep) is now war-risk and blockade enforcement — the 20% toll was scrapped within 24 hours. Friday 17-Jul's weekly is the first fully post-escalation print. Source: Platts via IndexBox. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: Latest public SMM weekly print (10-Jul): $355/t, down from $380 — assessed BEFORE the closure declaration, the tanker strikes, the blockade start and the toll's rise-and-fall. Friday 17-Jul's weekly is the first to carry the full sequence. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343 / SMM.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: Platts daily P1020A duty-UNPAID in-whs Rotterdam last publicly assessed $525–560/t (mid $542.50, 03-Jul) — no fresher public print. Structural floor: 6% EU duty + CBAM + Mozal (~580 ktpa) on care & maintenance. Gulf war-risk freight remains the live cost leg after the Omani-corridor strikes; the prospective 20% toll adder is gone (scrapped 14-Jul) (S&P Global / SMM).

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs duty-paid Rotterdam — LAST PUBLIC REFERENCE (late-Jun; no fresh public print). Conversion premia are stickier than ingot premia; Mozal remains down. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts US MW Transaction premium last publicly assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t (03-Jul). The tariff-$ leg moves MECHANICALLY with the LME (≈$1,585/t at the 14-Jul cash of $3,171 × 50%), so Tuesday's LME gain lifts the US all-in automatically. SMM's 10-Jul weekly had DDP at 109.75¢. Verify S&P Global.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: SMM weekly DDP print 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul, from 110.25¢) — assessed before the escalation week. Duty-paid = LME + Section-232 tariff (50%) + market premium; the policy leg is ~two-thirds of the all-in and rose ~$16/t mechanically with Tuesday's LME. The 20% toll never reached enforcement — scrapped 14-Jul. Verify Platts/Fastmarkets.

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Settled ~$84.73 Wednesday (essentially flat vs Tuesday) after Monday's +9.6% surge; live ~$85.6 into Thursday. Support: a second US strike wave on Iran (an empty tanker toward Kharg Island disabled), the Iranian-port blockade in force, and traffic through Hormuz down to a trickle. The 20% strait toll stays scrapped. Energy is the largest smelter cost line.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: DXY ~100.5 (−0.4%), extending the post-CPI slide after a second soft inflation print (June PPI −0.3% m/m). July hike odds ~20%, September ~60% (Bloomberg swaps). Fed Chair Warsh's testimony leaned against the dovish read but did not reverse it. A softer dollar removes the headwind that capped metals — supportive at the margin even as fundamentals soften.

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Euro firmer ~1.146 on the broad dollar slide (Westmetall LME-FX 1.1407 at the 14-Jul fixing). ECB still carries a war-energy inflation problem with Brent at $85. A stronger euro lifts EU-denominated smelter power and conversion costs.

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.78. The June data mix is aluminium-bearish: RECORD unwrought-aluminium exports of 711 kt (+12.5% m/m; H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3% y/y — GACC via Reuters) while this morning's Q2 GDP missed at 4.3% y/y (slowest since 2022) with retail sales up just 1.0% — production strong, domestic demand weak, exports the release valve.

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen ~162, marginally firmer on the US yield drop. A still-weak yen cushions Japan-bound cargo premia in local terms; the binding Q4-talks variable remains Gulf transit risk — now minus the toll but plus an enforced blockade.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: 10Y ~4.55% as two cool inflation prints (CPI −0.4%, PPI −0.3%) pulled July hike odds to ~20%. BoC held at 2.25% Wednesday. Real rates remain the macro cap on metals; a hot data surprise re-arms the dollar.

macro_events: SECOND COOL INFLATION PRINT: US June PPI (Wed 08:30 ET) fell 0.3% m/m — final-demand goods −1.4% (biggest drop since Jul-2022, led by a ~12% gasoline slump), services +0.2%; +5.5% y/y unadjusted. Coming after Tuesday's CPI −0.4%, it hardens the disinflation read: July Fed-hike odds ~20%, dollar softer, 10Y ~4.55% (BLS via bls.gov; CNBC).

OFFICIAL CONFIRMED, THEN FLAT PRICE WOBBLED: Tuesday's 14-Jul LME official rebuilt the +$3.50 backwardation on a 284,600 t stock base; Wednesday's live 3M/CFD eased ~0.9% to ~$3,147.50 as the macro turned friendlier and record Chinese metal weighed. Westmetall has not yet posted the 15-Jul official — the confirmation-vs-fade question is unresolved.

POLICY: The 20% Strait-of-Hormuz toll stays scrapped (IMO called mandatory tolls illegal; shippers revolted), replaced by 'trade and investment deals' — but the maritime blockade of Iranian ports holds and a second US strike wave hit Iran Wednesday (CENTCOM; CNN live 15-Jul). War-risk freight/insurance stays elevated; the flat 20% cost line is gone.

BANK OF CANADA HELD at 2.25% (Wed) — growth picking up, inflation projected to ease from its war-driven gasoline spike (CPI 3.2% May; ex-gasoline 2.2%). A steady-hands G7 central bank against a backdrop of two soft US prints (bankofcanada.ca).

CHINA: Q2 GDP +4.3% y/y (slowest since 2022) with June industrial production +5.3% but retail sales +1.0% — production-heavy, demand-light, the configuration that keeps the record 711 kt export valve open (NBS via CNBC/FXStreet).
lme_commentary: The confirmation came Tuesday, and Wednesday tested it. The 14-Jul official printed cash $3,171.00 (+1.04%) / 3M $3,167.50 (+0.65%) and the cash–3M spread flipped from Monday's −$8.50 contango to +$3.50 BACKWARDATION — the first prompt-metal premium since the IRGC closure declaration, on a 284,600 t stock base (−44.1% YTD). But as of this 16-Jul run Westmetall has NOT yet tabled the 15-Jul ring, and the live 3M/CFD already gave back most of the bounce, easing ~0.88% Wednesday to ~$3,147.50. The reason is a macro that turned decisively disinflationary — June PPI −0.3% followed CPI −0.4% — colliding with a physical valve wide open: China exported a record 711 kt in June into its slowest growth since 2022. The technical read is unchanged: the flat price must hold above the $3,138 wave-ii shelf and reclaim $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382) to validate the catch-up; a slide back through $3,138 toward $3,085/$3,050 says Wednesday's fade was the tell. The single most informative print this week is now the delayed 15-Jul official: does the backwardation BUILD, or does the flat-price pullback drag the front end back toward contango?

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$340/t FOB East Australia — latest public reference. The global surplus story is intact (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarted 10-Jul; Nalco guides $320–330 for 2026) and Rio's Q2 report shows upstream volumes recovering — but the DELIVERED leg into the Gulf still carries war-risk cover after the Omani-corridor strikes (the 20% toll threat died 14-Jul). Verify SMM/Fastmarkets.

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$304/t — easing on the building surplus (latest public reference). SMM flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June on geopolitical and energy pressure — a partial offset to the EGA restart. Verify Platts.

alumina.name: SHFE alumina futures (AO2609)
alumina.src: Last public futures print in the ¥2,700s (SMM); price below all moving averages, port stocks building. SMM's domestic spot weighted average ~¥2,552/t. The contested strait still squeezes GULF refinery feed (~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz) even as the global balance stays long (SMM).

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$309/t — CME/TradingView; no material move in the latest public print.

alumina_notes: The alumina market remains the mirror image of metal: globally LOOSENING (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa restart, Guinea bauxite oversupply at ~$68/dmt CIF China, Chinese port stocks building; domestic China spot ~¥2,552 and drifting) — ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band still holds.

The regional wrinkle evolved this week: the strait remains contested — Iranian closure declaration, enforced US blockade, fatal strikes inside the Omani corridor — so the ~60% of Gulf alumina imports that transit Hormuz still face elevated war-risk cover; the threatened 20% toll, however, was scrapped within 24 hours. A regional feed squeeze inside a global surplus. SMM separately flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June.

Guinea's pending export-quota policy (targeting >$100/dmt bauxite) remains the one clear bullish catalyst on the commodity leg; Metro Mining's record 1.8 Mt Q2 shipments underline how long the seaborne market currently is.
net_read: THE METAL CONFIRMED, THEN BLINKED. Tuesday's official (+1.04% to $3,171) flipped the front spread to +$3.50 BACKWARDATION on the thinnest stocks of the cycle — physical endorsement of the blockade. Wednesday the flat price gave most of it back (~−0.9% on the live 3M to ~$3,148) as a second soft US print (PPI −0.3%) and a RECORD 711 kt of June Chinese exports reasserted the supply reality. The official confirmation and the live-price fade now sit in tension, and Westmetall has yet to post the 15-Jul ring to break the tie. Net: burden of proof is balanced — a building backwardation on the delayed official plus firm premium weeklies Friday still validates the path to $3,230/$3,260; a fade to contango while record Chinese metal lands, with Alcoa's Gulf-cost commentary tonight, tilts it back to the bears. Situational awareness, not advice.

feed.text: EUROPEAN MORNING CHECK: Westmetall has NOT yet tabled a 15-Jul official — the latest published ring remains Tue 14-Jul (Al cash $3,171.00 / 3M $3,167.50 / stock 284,600 t; spread +$3.50 backwardation). On a live/CFD basis the 3M eased ~0.88% Wednesday to ~$3,147.50, giving back most of Tuesday's bounce. Today's officials print 13:20 London, after this run.

US JUNE PPI −0.3% m/m (08:30 ET): final-demand goods −1.4% (biggest since Jul-2022, gasoline ~−12%), services +0.2%; +5.5% y/y. A second cool print after Tuesday's CPI −0.4% — reinforcing disinflation, softening the dollar (DXY ~100.5), and capping the flat price via the record-export supply story rather than lifting it (BLS/CNBC).

HORMUZ — SECOND STRIKE WAVE: the US said fresh strikes on Iran Wednesday were aimed at protecting vessels in the strait; CENTCOM disabled an empty oil tanker sailing toward Kharg Island. The blockade of Iranian ports holds; the 20% transit toll stays scrapped. Brent settled ~$84.73 (flat), live ~$85.6 (CNN live 15-Jul / CNBC).

BANK OF CANADA HELD at 2.25% — growth improving, inflation seen easing from its gasoline-driven spike (May CPI 3.2%, ex-gasoline 2.2%). A steady G7 hold amid two soft US inflation prints (bankofcanada.ca).

CHINA Q2 GDP +4.3% y/y — slowest since 2022; June IP +5.3% beat, retail +1.0% soft. Production-heavy, demand-light: the mix that keeps the record 711 kt monthly export flow running into the ex-China market (NBS via CNBC/FXStreet).

THE OFFICIAL THAT CONFIRMED: Tuesday's 14-Jul ring flipped the cash–3M spread to +$3.50 BACKWARDATION from −$8.50 contango on a 284,600 t stock base — the first prompt-metal premium since the closure declaration, struck with the 20% toll still live and record China exports on the tape (Westmetall).

AHEAD TONIGHT: Alcoa Q2 after the NYSE close (consensus ~$2.24 EPS / ~$4.12bn rev) — the first producer P&L struck under the blockade: watch Gulf freight/insurance, alumina economics and US Midwest realisation under the 50% Section-232 tariff (AlphaStreet/Benzinga).

news.headline: CONFIRMED, THEN FADED: Tuesday's 14-Jul official rebuilt a +$3.50 backwardation, but Wednesday's live 3M/CFD slipped ~0.88% to ~$3,147.50, giving back most of the bounce as CPI+PPI disinflation and record China exports weighed. Westmetall has yet to post the 15-Jul official — the confirmation-vs-fade question is unresolved.

SECOND SOFT US PRINT: June PPI −0.3% m/m (goods −1.4%, gasoline ~−12%) after CPI −0.4% — disinflation confirmed, July hike odds ~20%, dollar softer. Friendlier for risk, but for aluminium the offset is a wide-open Chinese export valve, so the net flat-price impulse is muted.

SECOND STRIKE WAVE, BLOCKADE HOLDS: the US struck Iran again Wednesday to protect strait shipping (empty tanker toward Kharg Island disabled); the Iranian-port blockade stays in force and the 20% toll stays scrapped. War-risk freight/insurance elevated; Brent ~$85.

RECORD EXPORTS, WEAK GROWTH: China's June aluminium exports hit a record 711 kt (first >700 kt; H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3% y/y) while Q2 GDP slowed to 4.3%. More surplus metal into the ex-China market exactly as it tightens on Gulf disruption.

ALCOA Q2 TONIGHT: after the NYSE close, consensus ~$2.24 EPS / ~$4.12bn rev — the first producer P&L under the blockade regime. Key reads: Gulf freight/insurance, alumina economics, and US Midwest premium realisation under the 50% tariff.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián restart completed Apr 2026. 01-Jul: agreed to acquire South32's bauxite/alumina/aluminium portfolio for $4.1bn upfront (up to $5.6bn contingent to 2030), close expected H1-2027. Q2 results TODAY, Thu 16-Jul after the NYSE close (call 5pm EDT); Street consensus EPS $2.24 (vs $0.39 a year ago) on revenue ~$4.12bn — the first producer P&L assessed under the blockade regime; watch war-freight/insurance and US Midwest realisation under the 50% tariff (BusinessWire/AlphaStreet/Benzinga).

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due 22-Jul (07:00 CEST).

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: H1 copper-equivalent output +3% y/y; aluminium division improved; strong bauxite recovery; FY aluminium guidance UNCHANGED at 3.25–3.45 Mt; Pilbara Q2 shipments 89 Mt (+5%).
earnings.readthrough: First upstream print of the Q2 season (this morning): Western volumes are intact — the Gulf disruption is expressing itself in prices, freight and premia rather than output. Kitimat/AP60 ramp continues; H1 financial results due 13-Aug.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings.headline: Guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154.4–175.6% YoY.
earnings.readthrough: Credits industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices fluctuating at highs, high-value-added deep-processing mix and energy-saving retrofits — the first sizeable China H1 pre-announcement of the season and a positive read-across for Chinese producer margins.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each listed peer's most recent reported results as of 16-Jul (public, listed-company information). Q2 season is OPEN: Rio Tinto's production report landed 15-Jul (volumes intact, aluminium guidance unchanged); Alcoa reports TODAY, Thu 16-Jul after the NYSE close — consensus EPS $2.24 on ~$4.12bn revenue (AlphaStreet/Benzinga); Norsk Hydro 22-Jul (07:00 CEST); Maaden (1211) H1 late-Jul/early-Aug (Tadawul). Corporate action: Alcoa–South32 upstream transaction ($4.1bn upfront, up to $5.6bn contingent, close H1-2027). Context for the prints: Q2 average LME cash ≈ $3,459 (June mean) versus $3,171 at the latest official — sequentially strong realised prices with a fading tail. The two disclosures that matter most: Gulf insurance/freight costs, and US Midwest premium realisation under the 50% tariff.

commercial.text: Tuesday confirmed (+1.04% official, +$3.50 backwardation on 284,600 t); Wednesday's live 3M gave most of it back (~−0.9% to ~$3,148). The discipline now is trend-vs-fade on the delayed 15-Jul official: a build in the backwardation with the flat price holding above $3,138 validates the path to $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382); a slide back to contango while record Chinese metal (711 kt June) lands confirms the fade. $3,050 the structural line. Technical context, not advice.

Every public premium print (MJP Q3 settled $395; spot $355; US MW DDP 109.75¢; EDU $542.5 mid) STILL pre-dates the full escalation — Friday 17-Jul's SMM/Platts weeklies are the first to price closure + blockade + strikes, minus the toll. The tariff-$ leg of US premiums moves mechanically with the LME (~$1,585/t at 50%). Watch Alcoa tonight for realised US Midwest and Gulf-freight colour.

Alumina stays globally long (EGA ramping, FOB Australia ~$340, API ~$304, China spot ~¥2,552) and Rio's Q2 report confirms upstream volumes intact (bauxite recovering, aluminium guidance 3.25–3.45 Mt unchanged). Gulf-delivered costs improved at the margin — no 20% toll — but corridor missile risk still prices into war-risk cover on ~60% of regional alumina import routings. Secondary feed stays the quiet tightener: UAE scrap export ban, EU tariff changes, China's reverse-invoicing suspension (SMM). Watch index-vs-delivered divergence.

Re-set the cost stack after the whiplash: (1) war-risk quotes stay wide and short-validity after the fatal Omani-corridor strikes and six fresh port-area strikes — this is now the dominant policy-risk line; (2) the 20% toll is DEAD — remove it from delivered-cost scenarios, but note the substitute ('trade and investment deals') is unpriced and the IMO-vs-Washington legal question is unresolved; (3) blockade enforcement (from 16:00 ET Tue) adds interdiction/delay risk for anything with an Iranian nexus. Friday's assessed premia are the first to price this stack; quotes off last week's assessments remain stale.

bottom_line: On 16-Jul the market sits between two prints. Behind it, Tuesday's official confirmed the war story — a rebuilt +$3.50 backwardation on the thinnest stocks of the cycle. In front of it, Wednesday's flat price already faded ~0.9% as a second soft US inflation read (PPI −0.3% after CPI −0.4%) and a record 711 kt of Chinese June exports reasserted the supply reality, and Westmetall has yet to post the 15-Jul official to settle which signal wins. Nothing has converged on the fundamentals: prompt tightness (backwardation, −44% YTD stocks, Gulf disruption) pulls one way; a wide-open Chinese export valve into 4.3% growth pulls the other. The next three prints price the tie-break — the delayed 15-Jul official, Alcoa's Q2 tonight (first producer P&L under the blockade), and Friday's post-blockade premium weeklies plus the US Treasury Iranian-oil deadline.
sources: LME official settlements & stock (14-Jul)

caveats: Report refreshed Thu 16-Jul-2026 ~06:50 London — BEFORE today's LME ring. The latest PUBLISHED official is Tue 14-Jul (cash $3,171.00 / 3M $3,167.50 / stock 284,600 t — Westmetall). As of this run Westmetall has NOT yet tabled a 15-Jul official, so the confirmed benchmark is unchanged from the prior run; the independent live/CFD 3M (TradingEconomics) eased ~0.88% on 15-Jul to ~$3,147.50, so the confirmed official pre-dates Wednesday's PPI print and the pullback. Today's officials print 13:20 London, after this run.

Live/CFD reads (aluminium ~$3,147.50 on 15-Jul; Brent, DXY, EUR/USD) are indicative market prices from public trackers and are a different series from LME officials — they are never mixed into the official benchmark rows. The base-metals board carries the latest published 14-Jul LME officials on a uniform basis (3M $/t; day % = 14-Jul vs 13-Jul; YTD vs 02-Jan); the 15-Jul cross-metal live moves (Zn −1.3%, Ni +0.5%, Pb −0.8%) are noted in commentary, not merged into the official board.

Premiums, alumina and the overseas physical complex show the latest PUBLIC prints (SMM weekly 10-Jul; Platts marks 03/06-Jul) — ALL assessed BEFORE the 12-Jul closure declaration, the 13-Jul strikes, the blockade and the toll episode. They therefore lag the current delivered-cost picture; Friday 17-Jul's weeklies are the first post-blockade assessments. Subscription indices (Platts/Fastmarkets) should be verified for any transaction.

Everything published here is PUBLIC market information only — no confidential, internal or company-specific data. The only company-specific mentions are public listed-company facts in the peer-earnings table (Alcoa, Norsk Hydro, Century, Rio Tinto, Maaden (1211)). Nothing here is trading, hedging or investment advice; Elliott Wave and technical notes are analytical context, not recommendations.

Geopolitical facts (US strikes, blockade, toll scrapped, Hormuz traffic) are compiled from public reporting (CNN/CNBC/NPR/Al Jazeera) and evolve intraday; figures were current at ~06:50 London on 16-Jul and may have moved by the time of reading.

forward_path.basis: Latest PUBLISHED official (Westmetall); +1.04% vs 13-Jul — a new recovery high, struck with the toll still live and record China exports on the tape, 10 minutes before the soft CPI. Today's official (13:20 London) tests whether the backwardation builds.
Latest published official; +0.65% vs 13-Jul; front spread +$3.50 BACKWARDATION (from −$8.50 contango). LME.com day-delayed 3M close corroborates at $3,169.50 (+0.96%).

outlook.curve_note: The diagnostic answered YES — provisionally. Monday's −$8.50 contango flipped to a +$3.50 BACKWARDATION on Tuesday's official, the first prompt-metal premium since the closure declaration, on a stock base of 284,600 t (−44.1% YTD). Read literally: the physical front end finally endorsed the blockade story — and did so before the CPI relief and before the toll was scrapped, i.e. on supply physics rather than macro. The counterweight is quantified at record scale: China exported 711 kt in June (first month ever >700 kt) and grew 4.3% in Q2 (slowest since 2022) — the export valve is wide open into a soft season. So the curve question rolls forward one day sharper: a second, wider backwardation today says tightness is a trend; a snap back to contango says Tuesday was a headline print and supply repair still governs. The 2026 consensus band ($3,200–3,500 averages) and the ~−365 kt structural deficit remain the floor beneath the argument.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; record export flow

scenarios.drivers: The blockade regime entrenches — enforcement incidents multiply, the Omani corridor stays unsafe, and the rebuilt backwardation extends on a sub-285kt stock base while the dollar stays soft post-CPI
drivers_list: Geopolitics: no Iranian 'strait open' statement and no Oman two-corridor agreement; blockade enforcement (live since 16:00 ET 14-Jul) produces interdiction incidents; strikes on Iranian ports continue — ~25% of ex-China supply stays hostage
Curve confirmation: Tuesday's +$3.50 backwardation BUILDS on subsequent officials — the tight-prompt configuration (backwardation + stocks −44.1% YTD) historically precedes flat-price catch-up
Macro tailwind NEW this week: CPI fell 0.4% m/m, July hike odds halved to ~20%, the dollar was the worst major — the rates ceiling that capped rallies has lifted for now
Premiums: Friday's first post-blockade weeklies print HIGHER on war-risk pass-through, arresting the Q3 slide and re-anchoring Q4 MJP talks at/above $395
Targets: $3,230/$3,260 fast on confirmation; a closing break of $3,260 opens $3,330–3,400; Citi/Goldman ~$3,600; $4,000+ only on a sustained (30-day+) interdiction of Gulf loadings

scenarios.drivers: A managed-transit equilibrium emerges — the 'trade and investment deals' pivot matures into a corridor arrangement, cargo moves at elevated but insurable cost, and record Chinese exports cap the war premium
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the toll walk-back is the tell — Washington prefers deals to escalation; Muscat-track diplomacy converges on managed transit (southern corridor free, northern under approval); sporadic incidents keep a front-of-curve risk premium without stopping flow
Supply: the ~−365 kt 2026 deficit persists but repairs visibly — EGA alumina back, cells restarting, RECORD 711 kt June Chinese exports, Indonesia ramping, Rio volumes intact
China: Q2 GDP 4.3% (slowest since 2022) with retail sales +1.0% — domestic softness keeps the export valve open; destocking supports the SHFE bottom but not a breakout
Macro: CPI relief holds but Warsh keeps September live (~60% priced) — DXY ~101 stops falling; the dollar neither caps nor fuels the metal
Premiums: weeklies firm modestly on war-risk; Q4 MJP negotiates in the $340–410 band around the $395 anchor

scenarios.drivers: De-escalation or a macro re-shock — either the deals pivot yields a corridor agreement and the war premium unwinds into record supply, or hot PPI/Warsh re-arms the hike trade and the dollar does the damage
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the 'trade and investment deals' framework matures fast — an Iranian 'strait open' statement or an agreed Oman corridor plan restores flow; the June pattern (war premium unwinding in days) repeats
Macro: today's PPI runs hot or Warsh's second day of testimony re-prices September (~60% already) toward a done deal — DXY back through 101.5 and metals de-rate as a complex
Supply: the record export machine (711 kt June, H1 +16.3%) keeps metal flowing into the off-season; EGA beats its ramp; premium weeklies print LOWER again despite the headlines
Physical: Tuesday's backwardation proves a one-day wonder — a snap back to contango would say the front end never truly repriced
Technical: a daily close below $3,138 weakens the recovery; below $3,065 re-opens $2,990–3,020, then the .618 at $2,894

risks.risk: Hormuz restriction entrenches: Iranian closure + enforced US blockade + strikes on six Iranian ports (~25% of ex-China supply routes)
Southern (Omani) corridor unusable after fatal missile strikes — no functioning workaround
Rapid de-escalation: the 'trade and investment deals' pivot yields a corridor agreement / Iranian 'strait open' statement — war premium unwinds in days
Physical looseness at record scale: China June exports 711 kt (first >700 kt), Q2 GDP miss, EGA restart, off-season demand
Disinflation entrenches: two soft US prints (CPI −0.4%, PPI −0.3%) cut July hike odds to ~20%; a softer dollar supports metals but signals demand weakness
US Section-232 at 50% distorts trade flows and US premium realisation (tariff leg ≈$1,585/t and rising with the LME)

catalysts.event: The delayed 15-Jul LME official (pending on Westmetall) — does the +$3.50 backwardation BUILD or fade after Wednesday's ~0.9% live-price pullback? Plus Alcoa Q2 after the NYSE close (consensus EPS $2.24 / rev ~$4.12bn) — first producer P&L under the blockade: war freight, insurance, Gulf logistics, US Midwest realisation under the 50% tariff

First post-blockade premium weeklies (SMM/Platts) — the first assessments to price closure + strikes + blockade, minus the toll — AND the US Treasury Iranian-oil wind-down deadline. The real premium verdict
Norsk Hydro Q2 (07:00 CEST) — European producer read: power costs, Extrusions demand, premium realisation
Maaden (1211) H1 results (Tadawul) — Gulf producer read on realised prices and volumes through the disruption
MJP Q4 settlement talks open against the $395 decade-high anchor — the pending-quarter range ($345–425) resolves on blockade duration and the Q3 spot-vs-settlement gap

An agreed Oman two-corridor mechanism or an Iranian 'strait open' statement — the fastest unwind trigger; conversely a blockade-enforcement incident with a non-Iranian vessel is the fastest escalation trigger

ai_analysis: 16-Jul's defining tension is a split signal: Tuesday's official CONFIRMED the war story (front spread +$3.50 backwardation on 284,600 t stocks), yet Wednesday's live 3M FADED ~0.9% to ~$3,148 as the macro turned disinflationary (PPI −0.3% after CPI −0.4%) and record Chinese metal reasserted itself. Westmetall's delayed 15-Jul official is the tie-breaker.

The bear case keeps its best number of the year: 711 kt of Chinese exports in June — the first month ever above 700 kt — into an economy growing at its slowest since 2022. The ex-China market is tightening on Gulf disruption at the exact moment China has record metal to send it. Both facts are true; the flat price has to clear both.

The policy lesson stands: Washington's maximalism has a 24-hour half-life when industry pushes back (the 20% toll died fast), but the enforcement risk it leaves behind — blockade, repeated strikes, war-freight/insurance — is stickier than any headline levy. That keeps a structural premium in delivered cost even as the flat price wobbles.

Near term the burden of proof is balanced. A building backwardation on the delayed official, constructive Alcoa Gulf-cost commentary tonight, and firmer premium weeklies Friday would validate the catch-up toward $3,230/$3,260. A fade back to contango with the flat price losing $3,138 tilts the odds to a retest of $3,085/$3,050.

premium_explainer.desc: The physical-delivery adder over the LME price: regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness — quoted $/t (or ¢/lb in the US) on top of LME cash. It is what converts an exchange price into a delivered price.

Q3-2026 settled at $395/t CIF Japan (Platts, 06-Jul) — a decade high and the fourth straight rise ($86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). SMM's 10-Jul weekly had SPOT at $355 — below the settlement, assessed before the closure declaration and the escalation week. Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor, an enforced US blockade and a contested strait — but no transit toll (scrapped 14-Jul).

Platts duty-unpaid last publicly marked $525–560 (mid $542.50, 03-Jul), down ~$70 from mid-May as the war-spike unwound. The 6% EU duty, CBAM and Mozal's care & maintenance keep the structural floor — and the war-risk freight component has flipped from easing to re-widening after the fatal Omani-corridor strikes. The prospective 20% toll layer died on 14-Jul.

SMM's weekly DDP print eased to 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul); Platts MW Transaction last printed 108.7¢ (03-Jul). The 50% Section-232 tariff-$ leg (≈$1,585 at the 14-Jul LME cash) dominates the all-in — US premia track Washington and the LME, not local tightness.

The decomposition below separates the mechanical tariff leg (Section-232 rate × LME) from the residual market premium. Most of the US 'premium' is a policy transfer, not a scarcity signal — and the new 20% strait toll extends the same logic to the freight leg of any Gulf-origin cargo, US-bound or not.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 settled $395 (decade high) but spot printed $355 before the escalation. Friday 17-Jul's weekly is the first assessment to carry the closure, the corridor strikes, the blockade AND the toll's rise-and-fall; if the two-sided restriction holds, the slide stalls and Q4 talks (~Sep) anchor at or above the settlement; a Muscat/Oman corridor deal — now the explicit US preference given the 'trade deals' pivot — reopens the path to the LOWER half of the $340–410 band.

European (EDU): last public Platts mark $525–560 (03-Jul). The 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal's outage keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war; the marginal driver — Gulf war-risk freight — stays wide after missiles hit tankers INSIDE the Omani corridor, though the threatened 20% toll never became a cost line (scrapped within 24 hours).

US Midwest: SMM DDP 109.75¢/lb (10-Jul); the tariff leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,585 at the 14-Jul cash) is ~two-thirds of the all-in, so US premia track the LME and Washington. Tuesday's LME gain lifted the US all-in mechanically, and the CPI-driven dollar slide removes a macro headwind. Watch the tariff arithmetic and Friday's weekly, not the spot survey.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a RECORD 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% YoY; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held; SMM 08-Jul); Guinea–EGA settlement returns CBG flows while Guinea weighs export quotas toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Mining.com/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: surplus intact — EGA's restart ramps through H2 while SHFE AO sits below all moving averages; expect ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band (FOB Australia ~$340, API ~$304, Platts futures ~$309). SMM's June ex-China output dip (−6% y/y) and Guinea's quota push are the counterweights to watch.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38); record Australian shipments add length. EGA's ramp is 'subject to optimisation of bauxite supply chains' — its buying returns demand to the seaborne market even as Guinea weighs a >$100/dmt export-price push.

Caustic soda / lime / freight: caustic stable (~$500/t FOB NE Asia). The delivered-cost leg IS the story: war-risk on Gulf-bound reagent and alumina freight stays wide after the Omani-corridor strikes, though the 20% toll never took effect (scrapped 14-Jul). The commodity is cheap; insuring it into a Gulf refinery is not.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen. Energy complex still repricing: Brent $84.73 (+1.7% Tue, after Monday's +9.6% best-day-since-2020); EU TTF gas ~€53/MWh — gas-indexed smelter power follows with a lag. Rising carbon-cost exposure.

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-transparent leg of the input basket — Cu 3M $13,454.00 (10-Jul official, latest published at run time; the complex held steady through Monday's escalation while COMEX copper was flat). Busbar/cathode exposure prices off the LME.

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.val: Contested — Iranian closure + enforced US blockade (toll scrapped)
logistics.note: Iran's IRGC says the strait is closed 'until further notice'; the US maritime blockade of Iranian ports and coastal areas took effect 16:00 ET 14-Jul (CENTCOM), with a third consecutive night of strikes hitting six Iranian port/coastal targets — Bushehr, Chah Bahar, Jask, Konarak, Abu Musa, Bandar Abbas. The 20% transit toll was scrapped within 24 hours of its announcement. Kpler data show crossings down more than half week-on-week. ~80–85% of Gulf aluminium exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use this waterway (NPR / CNBC / Washington Times).

logistics.name: Southern (Omani) corridor
logistics.val: Attacked 13-Jul — no longer priced as safe
logistics.note: The JMIC had held the corridor 'operational'; hours later two UAE tankers (Mombasa, Al Bahiyah) were hit by Iranian cruise missiles INSIDE Omani territorial waters — one seafarer killed, eight injured (UAE MoD). Oman has drafted a two-corridor traffic plan (southern free; northern subject to Iranian approval) that is not yet agreed. Until it is, the last workaround carries live missile risk (BOE Report / TheStreet).

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: War-risk quotes stay wide and short-validity after the fatal 13-Jul strikes inside the 'safe' Omani lane and the fresh strikes on Iranian ports. The 20% US toll — briefly a ~$32m line item for a loaded VLCC — was withdrawn on 14-Jul after IMO and industry opposition; delivered-cost models should remove the flat toll adder but retain elevated insurance and blockade-enforcement delay risk (CNBC / The Hill).

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: 2,980 on 14-Jul (+0.68%, fourth consecutive gain — highest since 05-Jun; Capesize 4,751 +1%, Supramax at a post-2022 peak). BDI is rising on dry-bulk DEMAND (bauxite/alumina flows); war-risk and blockade-enforcement costs are separate, faster-moving lines. Price the legs separately (Trading Economics / Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News).

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: With the strait contested by both navies, the southern corridor attacked and a per-transit toll in force, overland trucking to Red Sea ports moves from contingency to live logistics: carrier capacity, costings and lead times need to be current. This is a generic supply-chain observation for any Gulf-origin cargo.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: A declared closure plus an enforced US blockade keeps force-majeure and deviation clauses live for any Gulf-routed cargo; the toll-allocation question died with the toll, but blockade-related delay/interdiction risk and war-risk surcharge pass-through remain unsettled contract points to negotiate cargo-by-cargo. Generic supply-chain observation for any Gulf-origin cargo.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (Brent $84.73 after two straight risk-bid sessions; EU TTF ~€53 — gas-indexed power follows with a lag), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, 3M $13,592 at the 14-Jul official).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.

Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China). New this week: the UAE implemented an aluminium SCRAP export ban and the EU raised scrap-relevant tariffs (SMM) — secondary-feed tightness is a rising cost-side theme.

Logistics is the input line that changed twice in 24 hours: the commodity legs (alumina, bauxite) stay cheap, and the threatened 20% transit toll is gone — but the DELIVERED leg into and out of the Gulf still carries missile risk inside the 'safe' corridor and blockade-enforcement delay risk. Cheap alumina you cannot insure at last week's rates is still not cheap alumina.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): unchanged count — the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) terminated at $3,855 on 02-Jun-2026 (an extended, war-driven fifth wave with confirmed bearish weekly RSI divergence), and the ABC correction (A $3,359 / B $3,406 / C $3,061.5 on 02-Jul) bottomed inside the $2,950–$3,110 confluence where the .50 retracement overlaps the prior fourth of lesser degree. Base case (≈55%): a new impulse off $3,061.5, with a close above $3,260 (.382 trigger) opening $3,400 then $3,620 over the forecast year — supported by −44% YTD LME stocks and Gulf disruption. Bull (≈20%): blockade entrenchment drives a direct run at the $3,800 prior high. Bear (≈25%): record Chinese exports and disinflation-signalled demand weakness cap the rebound and force a retest of the $2,950–2,980 zone. Invalidation of the bullish count: a weekly close below $2,950. Wednesday's ~0.9% flat-price fade is noise within the base case; confidence moderate pending the delayed 15-Jul official.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Swing (daily): from the 02-Jul low at $3,061.5 the market built a five-wave micro-impulse into $3,210.5 (wave i), corrected as a wave-ii to the .50 retrace at $3,138 (tagged twice and held on a settlement basis), and Tuesday delivered the provisional wave-iii signature — a +1.04% official to $3,171 with the front spread flipping to +$3.50 backwardation. Wednesday's live 3M fade to ~$3,147.50 is a shallow wave-iv style pullback that must hold the $3,138 shelf. Base case (≈55%): iv holds $3,138, then a wave-v probes $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382), where a close extends toward $3,330. Bear alternate (≈35%): a close below $3,138 negates the impulse and opens $3,085/$3,061.5 — the read if the delayed official confirms the CFD fade. Bull (≈10%): a gap-and-go straight through $3,260. Invalidation: daily close below $3,061.5. Confidence moderate — the delayed 15-Jul official is the decisive tell.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz: closure + enforced US blockade (the dominant driver)
premium_drivers.d: Iran's IRGC declared the strait closed 'until further notice' (12-Jul); the US maritime blockade of Iranian ports took effect 16:00 ET 14-Jul with a third consecutive night of strikes on six Iranian port/coastal targets. Kpler counts strait crossings down more than half week-on-week. ~80–85% of Gulf aluminium exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use this waterway — war-risk cover and delay risk are the live premium drivers.
src: NPR — The U.S. is back to blockading Iran as the Strait of Hormuz standoff escalates (14-Jul)

premium_drivers.t: The 20% toll is DEAD — a cost line removed, an off-ramp signalled
premium_drivers.d: Less than 24 hours after announcing a 20% levy on all strait cargo, Trump scrapped it — the IMO called mandatory tolls illegal and the shipping industry pushed back — substituting 'Trade and Investment Deals' with Gulf states. For delivered-cost models: remove the flat toll adder; keep elevated war-risk. The pivot to deal-making is also the clearest de-escalation signal of the week — the fastest premium-unwind trigger if it matures into a corridor arrangement.
src: CNBC — Trump abandons Strait of Hormuz fee as U.S. launches new Iran airstrikes (14-Jul)

premium_drivers.t: US Section-232 tariff at 50% — the mechanical leg of US premia
premium_drivers.d: The tariff-$ component of the US Midwest all-in is 50% × LME: ≈$1,585/t at the 14-Jul cash official of $3,171 — roughly two-thirds of the ~$2,420 DDP premium. It rises and falls with the exchange price regardless of physical tightness; Tuesday's +1.04% LME move added ~$16/t to the US policy leg by arithmetic alone.
src: Federal Register — Adjusting Imports of Aluminum and Steel into the United States (50% rate)

premium_drivers.t: EU duty + CBAM + Mozal keep the European floor
premium_drivers.d: The 6% MFN duty and CBAM carbon costs are structural adders under any Rotterdam duty-paid quote, and Mozal (~580 ktpa) remains on care & maintenance — Europe stays structurally tighter than pre-war even while the assessed premium drifts with the war-risk cycle.
src: European Commission — Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism

premium_settlement_src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settlement $395/t (assessed 06-Jul-2026), via IndexBox

so_what.line: With Westmetall yet to post a 15-Jul official, the last confirmed LME print remains Tuesday's cash $3,171 / +$3.50 backwardation — but the live 3M market already pulled back ~0.9% to ~$3,148 on Wednesday as a second cool US inflation print (PPI −0.3%) and record Chinese exports blunted the war bid; all eyes now turn to Alcoa's Q2 tonight, the first producer P&L struck under the blockade.
points: What moved: aluminium's live 3M/CFD fell ~0.88% Wednesday to ~$3,147.50 (from $3,175.60), giving back most of Tuesday's +1.04% official gain. The latest PUBLISHED LME official is still Tue 14-Jul (cash $3,171 / 3M $3,167.50 / stock 284,600 t, −44.1% YTD; spread +$3.50 backwardation) — Westmetall has not yet tabled the 15-Jul ring.

Macro turned decisively disinflationary: US June PPI −0.3% m/m (final-demand goods −1.4%, biggest drop since Jul-2022, gasoline −12%) followed Tuesday's CPI −0.4%. Two soft prints in a row; DXY ~100.5 (−0.4%), 10Y ~4.55%. The Bank of Canada held at 2.25%; Fed Chair Warsh's testimony leaned against the dovish read.

The bear file is intact: China's record June exports (711 kt, first month >700 kt; H1 +16.3% to 3.4 Mt) meet a Q2 GDP miss of 4.3% — more surplus metal chasing foreign buyers. Geopolitics stayed hot: a second US strike wave on Iran Wednesday (an empty tanker toward Kharg Island disabled), blockade of Iranian ports in force, Brent settling near $84.73 (live ~$85.6).

Watch this week: Alcoa Q2 TONIGHT after the NYSE close (consensus ~$2.24 EPS / ~$4.12bn rev) — war-freight, insurance and US Midwest realisation under the 50% tariff; Friday 17-Jul the first post-blockade premium weeklies (SMM/Platts) and the US Treasury Iranian-oil wind-down deadline; and whether Westmetall's 15-Jul official confirms the CFD's pullback or holds the backwardation.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
